title: Share of Caribbean reefs with Acropora corals present or dominant
subtitle: The share of coral reefs in the Caribbean where major reef-building group of corals, _Acropora_, was dominant or present. The declines show the loss of coral cover as a result of pressures including water pollution, disease outbreaks, and [coral bleaching](#dod:coral_bleaching).
caption:
Share of Caribbean reefs with Acropora corals present or dominant
The share of coral reefs in the Caribbean where major reef-building group of corals, Acropora, was dominant or present. The declines show the loss of coral cover as a result of pressures including water pollution, disease outbreaks, and coral bleaching.

This line chart tracks the percentage of Caribbean coral reefs where Acropora corals were either present or dominant from 1950 to 2007. The vertical axis measures the share of reefs from 0% to 100%, while the horizontal axis spans the years. Two distinct lines represent the data: a brown line for reefs where Acropora was "Present" and a blue line for reefs where it was "Dominant." In 1950, the share of reefs with Acropora present stood at approximately 90%, while the share where it was dominant was near 80%. Both metrics show a general downward trajectory over the subsequent decades. The "Present" line drops to roughly 65% by the mid-1960s, recovers slightly to near 75% around 1975, and then falls steadily to just under 20% by 2007. The "Dominant" line follows a similar but steeper decline, falling below 50% by 1955 and reaching a low of roughly 5% in the early 2000s before a minor rise to roughly 8% in 2007. The gap between the two lines widens significantly after 1980, showing that while Acropora remained present on some reefs, it ceased to be the dominant species on most. By the end of the timeline, the share of reefs where Acropora was dominant sits at its lowest visible point, well below the 20% mark. The data shows a consistent reduction in the prevalence of this coral group across the region over the 57-year period.
